files.SKILL.md:
# Memo — harnessed shell work

**To:** executing model  
**From:** policy layer  
**Re:** tasks where success is decided by rerunnable commands in a disposable environment

---

The brief and the workspace together are the only spec. Paraphrasing them into prettier
words you like better is how scope drifts.

---

## Before You Touch Files

Write down — in your scratch area or the first reply — four things:
the exact verifier the brief names or implies, the cwd you believe you are in,
the exact output path the task requires, and the single smallest change that could
make that verifier pass. If any of those four is still vague, you are not ready
to edit.

When the task requires you to work around or extend existing code, read its full
source first. Identify the exact mechanism in use — the specific pattern, parser,
or filter logic — before designing your solution. A solution built on a generic
assumption about how such code works will fail when the actual implementation
differs. Test your solution against the actual code in the actual environment.

---

## While You Work

When a command fails, the stderr tail is usually worth more than another speculative
read. Read it completely before you change three unrelated files.

Prefer absolute paths after the first honest listing. Prefer flags that keep
installers and CLIs non-interactive so the session does not hang waiting for a TTY.

If two attempts with the same command shape produce the same failure class, the third
attempt must change approach — not the same flags with different hope.

After writing any output file, confirm it exists at the exact required path and has
non-zero size before proceeding. After writing any structured output (CSV, JSON,
delimited text), read it back and verify the header, first row, last row, and total
row count match the task specification exactly — character-for-character on field
names, delimiters, and row order.

When parsing categorical fields from input data, use exact-match or word-boundary
logic. Substring matching risks counting entries that merely contain the target
string. Read a sample of the actual input files before writing parsing logic.

Apply date and time range boundaries exactly as the task states. Treat inclusive
bounds as inclusive on both ends unless told otherwise. Derive all date references
from the task description, not from system time, unless the task explicitly
instructs otherwise.

---

## Concurrency and Process Lifecycle

When implementing concurrent work execution:

Prefer async-native primitives over thread-based approaches. Thread pools do not
propagate cancellation to running work units; async primitives do.

Enforce a concurrency cap with a gate that is checked at the moment each work unit
attempts to start — not only at scheduling time. Work units beyond the cap must
queue. A unit that is queued but has not yet started must never start once shutdown
is in progress. Scheduling all coroutines into a single gather does not enforce
this; the gate must be evaluated per unit at start time.

Register termination signal handlers before starting any work. On signal receipt:
stop accepting new work immediately, cancel every in-flight unit, await each unit's
cleanup, then exit. Every work unit must have a cleanup path that executes whether
the unit completes normally or is cancelled — and that path must be observable
(logged or otherwise verifiable).

Exit codes must reflect actual outcome. Never suppress exceptions silently during
shutdown; surface them to stderr.

---

## Closing

Close only when you can paste the verifier output that shows success, or you have a
one-line statement of what remains blocked and why it is outside the brief's fence.

Confirm positive evidence of correct output. The absence of errors is not success.
